speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Rose Sher Weiss-- R-O-S-E, S-H-E-R, W-E-I-S-S. I'm interviewing Alfred Adler-- A-L-F-R-E-D, A-D-L-E-R-- on November 21st, 1995 in the English language in Tamarac, Florida, USA. Just tap me on the shoulder when you're ready. What is your name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell the last name?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth, Fred?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any other names?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your date of birth, Fred?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents live in Munich before you were born?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where they came from originally, your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: --the name. My father was born in Urspringen-- U-R-S-P-R-I-N-G-E-N. And my mother was born in Mansbach-- M-A-N-S-B-A-C-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your family support itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a horse and cattle wholesaler, selling mostly to the large breweries in Germany horses.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he worked out of Munich?

speaker: interviewee
text: Out of Munich and two stables outside of Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers, sisters?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what language was spoken inside your home?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what schools did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I started with the elementary school, Stieler School in Munich.

speaker: interviewee
text: S-T-I-E-L-E-R School. And then tried to graduated from Ludwig's Realschule in Munich, which was interrupted through our friend Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your family's place in the community? Was your father active?

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brings me to the next question, how would you categorize the religious background of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was brought up through my parents as an Orthodox Jewish boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Every Friday and Saturday and holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did your father have any political interests? Was he Zionist, Pro-Zionist? No?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you remember any anti-Semitism back when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I mean, on an off through school, through other school children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any religious anti-Semitism directed at your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that time that I can remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when did you first start feeling the onset of Hitler in Munich?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, obviously, we knew in 1933, being that in Munich was his headquarters. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So we knew, but we didn't expect it to be this bad at that time as it turned out later on to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the Beer House Putsch?

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Hitler living in Munich at the time?

speaker: interviewer
text: And when he drove through Munich, did he drive alone with a whole--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, always with an entourage.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember what happened with Roehm, R-O-E-H-M?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember, as the story goes, that Roehm was a homosexual who had a villa in the Bavarian Alps, to be exact, Bad Wiessee, and-- at which time, he had young boys sleeping with him. And one night, Hitler raided his house and drove him back in his limousine, in his open limousine, gun on his temple so everybody could see that Hitler wanted to shoot him right there and   then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Driving by, I saw it from the window of my-- of our apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when do you remember the first anti-Jewish problems that broke out in Munich?

speaker: interviewee
text: As much as I remember, it started with-- it started really in 1933. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] But this, of course, is vague in my memory. Then I know for sure when it really started was November the 9th--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. At which time, I drove late at night from Frankfurt to Munich. And through every town I drove through, I saw fires and didn't know the reason for it. I had no idea that they burned synagogues or prayer books or whatever. But it struck me funny that every town should have a large fire going. And when I drove home, my parents were on the window and told me that in the house across the street from us, the Gestapo is making raids and I should go and pick up my mother's sister, who had a large estate 30 kilometers outside of Munich, because they were threatened to burn the house down if she is not out within an hour, which I did. I drove out there, loaded up whatever I could, and drove back so that she could stay with my-- her sister. On arriving home, I was stopped by a little short guy who identified himself as Gestapo and told me, pull the car over on the side, give me your license, registration. We have to take you in for questioning. In two hours you will be back. So I had no choice doing that. He threw me into a paddy wagon, which was just enough room for one person to turn around. And he drove around with me for a couple of hours, at which time I could hear that more people got loaded in. But we still didn't know what's going on. At the destination, Gestapo headquarters, we had to jump out from the paddy wagon and got whipped with an oxtail. This was the greeting. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] At the interrogation at Gestapo headquarters, they made me sign a paper that they found weapons in the car, which I didn't want to sign. And I had a choice, having had the pistol on my temple or sign, so I signed it. A couple of hours later, we were put back in the paddy wagon. Nobody told us anything. And we are transported to Dachau.

speaker: interviewee
text: By Gestapo paddy wagons. At the arrival in Dachau, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] they ripped our clothes off, gave us a hot-- and I mean hot-- shower, almost burning, and then put on the prison uniform, which was more-- no more than a pajama, really. And then we had to stand approximately six hours in 24-degree cold after the hot shower, at which time they came and shaved our heads, or most of it, ripped the-- ripped the hair out. Before at night, we got into [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the barrack, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] at which time later on-- it must have been close to midnight-- I heard a voice which sounded like my father's. And I crawled around the barrack. And true-- they arrested him later on. And lucky enough, he was in the same barrack as I was put in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the number of that barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stube-- S-T-U-B-E-- 4. I shall-- shall never forget [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And this was the greeting in-- into Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was running the show in Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, all the SS, SS troops, shepherd dogs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were your fellow prisoners there?

speaker: interviewee
text: All Jewish people from all over, Germany mostly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the conditions like in Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they probably were not as bad at that time as later on in different camps. But you did not know from one minute to the next if you are alive or dead. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You could only do certain walkways. And if you stepped off the walkway for a yard or so, the tower guard had a right to shoot you. You could not-- and I want to make this very clear-- you could not escape from this camp. The camp was surrounded by electrically-loaded fences. If you came half a yard close to the fence, electricity pulled you to the fence, and you were out. You were dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: So of course, food [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was impossible. As I found out later on, we ate, most of the time rats.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] soup. Soup, I was told--

speaker: interviewee
text: --was quite a bit of urine. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: I was assigned to carry big-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: --which were extremely heavy. Two men had to carry it. And you had to watch out that you don't spill any drops, or you would have gotten a beating from the SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you were eating soup with urine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Not all the time, but most of the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the sanitary conditions there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible. They were open stalls, open-- open showers. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We had typhoid breaking out in the barrack. And we are isolated for a while, which was a blessing in disguise, because we had high snow at that time-- and being that we had to go for roll call twice a day. So this was 10 days of isolation, which were in our favor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in the infirmary when you had typhoid?

speaker: interviewee
text: They kept us in the barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. And then, of course, every morning, we had to pick up the dead bodies and bring them into a trench, which I did. What they did with it, obviously we don't know. But obviously, they were burned or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think these people died from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Malnourishment. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, there were a lot of old people who couldn't take the cold. And some might have had infectious diseases.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was released after three weeks, as there was a decree for all those people who fought in World War I and had the Iron Cross. On the day when he got out, I couldn't even talk to him no more. And of course, he knew how bad the conditions were in the camp. He was very worried when he got home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you suffer in Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I understand, my father knew somebody at the commando in Dachau. And he had to sell everything to bribe me out.

speaker: interviewer
text: He-- he sold your home, your furniture, and everything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not our apartment, no, but the business--

speaker: interviewee
text: And everything what-- what went with it. That's the story as I got it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go when you left Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went home. I was home for, oh, I would say probably three weeks or so working in the Jewish hospital in Munich as a volunteer for those people who came out from Dachau and had their frostbites or whatever treated at the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was treating them? What doctors? Jewish doctors?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did that go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: 'Til I was confronted one day to make up my mind within an hour, as somebody who was scheduled to leave for China died. And the hospital-- I believe in combination with the American [INAUDIBLE] or Jewish Federation, whatever it was-- offered me the passage to China.

speaker: interviewer
text: This person who died, do you know his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His passage to  get to China.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to China. And when was that, Fred?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it was in May of '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents were living in their home?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- still in their apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they feel about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Obviously, terrible. But I had no choice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get to Shanghai from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train and boat. By train from Munich to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Venizia.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Venice. And from Venice by an Italian ocean liner, to China.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, before you got on that boat, did you have any bureaucratic problems in Munich before you got out?

speaker: interviewee
text: With customs. And you only were allowed to take 10 marks with you, which was, of course, nothing. And everything had to be done within I believe three days at that time. And they made an exception doing it so that I can get out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I had a problem on the German-Italian border. They searched everything again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I don't know if they were soldiers or police or customs.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were the Germans who searched you?

speaker: interviewer
text: And you got on the boat. How long were you on the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I remember correctly, it was 21 days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the conditions like on the boat?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was on the boat? People going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly-- mostly refugees at that time, or mostly German Jewish people. But it was open to-- to everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was running that boat? I mean, who was in charge of that boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Italian line. I don't know what the-- what the name of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: And it took 21 days. Did you have food on the boat?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your contact with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vaccinations into the chest. It was about a 10-inch needle. Terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it was 21 days, stopping quite frequently.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first stop, I believe, was Brindisi.

speaker: interviewee
text: Brindisi in Italy. Second was Aden in Yemen. Massawa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get off the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sri Lanka today. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hong Kong. Shanghai.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got to Shanghai, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, being that I had experience in the Jewish hospital in Munich, there was a Hungarian fellow who was a multimillionaire. And he opened a hospital in Shanghai. And he asked for who has experience and would want to-- to help. I volunteered at that time. He took me. And I started to work the next day. The doctor at that time was an Austrian, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a head doctor, who is, to this very day still in Astoria. And he's about 90, 92, or 93 years young.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's still practicing to this very day.

speaker: interviewer
text: So worked in the hospital as a volunteer?

speaker: interviewee
text: This particular fellow, who his name was a Mr. Komor-- K-O-M-O-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did Mr. Komor live in Shanghai? Was he--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think in the French concession.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you work for the hospital?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you arrived in Shanghai, was it difficult to be processed by the Chinese? Did they--
